title: Romance of the Three Kingdoms / Chapter 3 — In Wenming Gardens Dong Zhuo Silences Ding Yuan / Li Su Bribes Lü Bu with Gold and Pearls
text:
Let us turn now to the dayCao Caosaid toHe Jin, “The disasters caused by eunuchs are the same from ancient times to now; it is merely a case of an earlier sovereign granting them inappropriate favor and authority which brings things to such a state. If you just want to correct the situation, you need only remove the chief villains, and for that, a single jailer would suffice. Why would you summon provincial troops one by one? On the other hand, trying to execute all of them would guarantee that the secret would get out. I reckon you would be bound for failure.”

He Jinangrily replied, “What scheme might you be plotting,Mengde?”1•••

Not a lot of trust forCao Caoand a return ofHe Jin’s arrogance.↩

Cao Caoleft the meeting, sighing, “The one who will plunge the realm into chaos is thatHe Jin!”

He Jinthus privately sent out agents bearing secret edicts, traveling day and night to every major garrison.

It will be recalled thatDong Zhuo, who served asGeneral of the Front,MarquisofAoxiang, andInspectorofLiang province, had been removed from command for having failed in his battles against theYellow Turbans. He would have been convicted by the court, butDong Zhuohad bought off theTen Regular Attendantsto escape prosecution. Later on, through forming ties with influential men at court,Dong Zhuohad received further conspicuous positions. He commanded an army of two hundred thousand men of the western provinces, and he often had an insubordinate heart.

Dong Zhuowas overjoyed upon receiving the imperial edict and set to organizing his army without delay. He assigned his son-in-lawNiu Fu, who wasHousehold Gentleman, to guard the rear at Shanxi while he set out with the rest of his forces, including his officersLi Jue,Guo Si,Zhang Ji, andFan Chou, on a march toLuo.

Another son-in-law, his adviserLi Ru, said to him, “Although we may be acting on official orders, there is still much room for misinterpretation of our intentions. Why not send someone on ahead to present a petition clarifying our aims and expressing our loyalty? Then we can succeed in our endeavor.”Dong Zhuowas very pleased by this advice, and he sent in a petition which went something like this:

I have heard that the thing preventing the end of disorder and treason across the realm are theRegular Attendants of the Yellow Gates,Zhang Rangand the rest, who are flouting and violating the natural order. As they say, although one can stop a pot from boiling through vigorous stirring, better to just remove the wood that heats it; although it is painful to burst an abscess, better that than to let it fester all the more. Thus I am presuming to blow the horns and beat the drums by marching intoLuo, and I ask permission to removeZhang Rangand his ilk. For the blessings of the state! For the blessings of therealm!2•••

An early version of the novel includedDong ZhuoaccusingZhang Rangof having a lot of prime farmland near the capital.↩

WhenHe Jinobtained this petition, he brought it out to show the chief ministers. The Imperial Clerk,Zheng Tai, objected, “ThisDong Zhuois a wild dog! If you bring him into the capital, he will feast on humanflesh!”3•••

Zhang Tai was a leader in the north, he refused office until Emperor Ling’s death and then joinedHe Jinon personal invitation, he had risen through ranks and become a trusted adviser.↩

ButHe Jinsaid, “You’re too paranoid, unfit to discuss greatschemes.”4•••

He Jinnot only rejects good advice, he scorns and insults those who give council of caution.↩

Lu Zhialso objected: “Long have I known this manDong Zhuo; he may have a fine face, but he is a wolf at heart. Letting him slip into the inner palace even once is sure to breed calamity. Better to put a stop to his coming to prevent bringing on adisaster.”5•••

Lu Zhiis the Han general who fought against the Turbans and wrongly arrested in chapter 1 and exonerated in chapter 2.↩

YetHe Jinwas obstinate. The two objectors resigned their posts and retired, with more than half the court ministers following suit.He Jinsent agents to welcomeDong Zhuoat Mianchi, whereDong Zhuodrew up his troops and made no furthermovement.6•••

Mianchi meantDong Zhuohad crossed the Yellow River and into the capital district, moving eastwards to the capital.↩

WhenZhang Rangand the others learned that these provincial troops had arrived, they said to one another, “This isHe Jin’s plot. Unless we make the first move, our clans will be exterminated.” Thus they first hid a band of fifty armed ruffians within theGate of Resplendent Virtueat Empress Dowager He’s residence in thePalace of Lasting Happiness, then went in to speak with her.

“TheGrand Generalhas forged an edict calling up provincial troops to the capital, seeking to eradicate us,” they explained to her. “Madam, take pity and grant us relief.”

“Why not go to the’s office and confess your faults?” she asked.

“If we go there, we would be cut to mincemeat,” saidZhang Rang. “Madam, we can only hope that you will summon him to the palace and command him to cease. And if he refuses, then we pray but to die in your presence.”

Empress Dowager He thus issued an edict summoningHe Jin. Upon receiving it,He Jinplanned to go to her at once, yetChen Linobjected. “For the Empress Dowager to have issued this edict meansThe Tenmust have put her up to it. Do not go, for if you do, you will court disaster.” ButHe Jinreplied, “It is the Empress Dowager who summons me. What disaster could therebe?”7•••

Chen Linwas a noted scholar and student ofCai Yong(from first chapter), another ofHe Jin’s personal hires. ThatHe Jinis ignoring. To find talent is one thing, to use it quite another.↩

Yuan Shaosaid, “The plan has already leaked and the plot is already known. General, would you really still enter the palace like this?”

AndCao Caoremarked, “You should first summon the Ten out of the palace before going in.”

ButHe Jinlaughed and said, “Such childish perspectives! I have the power of the whole realm in the palms of my hands! Would the Ten dare to mistreat methen?”•••

He Jinremains obstinate, even when his allies warn him about the eunuchs’ trap.

Yuan Shaosaid, “Sir, if you insist on going, we will bring soldiers along as an escort to guard against whatever might happen.”

And soYuan ShaoandCao Caoeach selected five hundred veterans, orderingYuan Shao’s younger brotherYuan Shuto leadthem.8•••

Yuan Shuwas a noted hunter and falconer who made a name for himself as a gallant. Had held several posts at court↩

Yuan Shu, clad in full armor, drew up the men outside theGreen Chain GatewhileYuan ShaoandCao Caobuckled on their swords and accompaniedHe Jinto the front of thePalace of Lasting Happiness. There a eunuch attendant informed them, “TheEmpress Dowagerhas summoned only the Grand General. The others are not permitted to enter.” And soYuan Shao,Cao Cao, and the rest remained outside the palacegates.•••

Well that security measure has a bit of a flaw in.

He Jinproudly strolled right in. But when he reached theGate of Resplendent Virtue,Zhang RangandDuan Guiemerged to confront him as their fellows encircled him.He Jinwas astonished.

Zhang Rangrebuked him in a stern voice. “What crime had Empress Dowager Dong committed, that you so rashly poisoned her to death? And when theMother of the Statewas buried, you claimed illness to not bother leaving your estate! You were just a brat from a family of butchers until we recommended you to theSon of Heavenand brought you glory and prestige, yet instead of trying to show your gratitude, here you are plotting to do us in! You call us so much filth, and yet just who is the clean one?”

In a panic,He Jinlooked about for a way to escape, but the palace gates had all been closed and the ruffians had come out of hiding, and they choppedHe Jinin two. As a later poet lamented:

Wobbly was that house of Han

When witlessHe Jinheld the reins

No ear hath he for blunter words

Yet takes a sharper point9•••

A big character dying can get a poem. They are not always very positive. The rise of such a lowly family is used as an example of the Han’s decline while his personal failings are strongly highlighted.↩

WithZhang Rangand the others having killedHe Jin,Yuan Shaowaited for a long time without seeing him emerge. Eventually, he called through the gate, “General, your carriage awaits!” In response,Zhang Rangand the rest pitched the head ofHe Jindown from the wall.

The eunuchs announced, “He Jinwas plotting rebellion, but he has already been put to death. His followers are hereby pardoned and forgiven.” ButYuan Shaofuriously shouted, “The eunuchs have conspired and killed a chief minister! Let those who will punish this wicked faction come and help me fight!”

One ofHe Jin’s officers,Wu Kuang, promptly began spreading a fire outside theGreen Chain Gate.Yuan Shuat the head of his men burst into the palace complex and fell to slaying any eunuch they could find, without regard to age or rank.Yuan ShaoandCao Caobroke into the inner part of the palace, from where four ofThe Ten,Zhao Zhong,Cheng Kuang,Xia Yun, andGuo Sheng, were brought to the front of theBlue Flower Lodgeand hacked to pieces. The fire spread within the palace, the smoke blotting out the sky.

Another four ofThe Ten,Zhang Rang,Duan Gui,Cao Jie, andHou Lan, made off with Empress Dowager He,the Emperor, and thePrinceofChenliu, fleeing along a back road toward the Northern Palace.10•••

ThePrinceofChenliuis the Emperor Shao’s younger brother↩

AlthoughLu Zhihad resigned his position, he had not yet departed the capital. Hearing that there was some commotion in the palace, he had donned his armor, grabbed his spear, and waited beneath a pavilion. From a distance he spiedDuan Guihurrying Empress Dowager He along and called out, “Duan Gui, you rebel, how dare you abduct the Empress Dowager?”Duan Guiat once turned and fled. Empress Dowager He leaped out of a window, andLu Zhicame to her rescue and rushed her to safety.

Wu Kuangburst into the inner courtyard, where he foundHe Miao, sword in hand.

Wu Kuangexclaimed, “He Miaowas part of this plot to kill his brother! He should die like the others!”

And the soldiers cried out, “Let’s take the head of that traitor who schemed against his brother!”

He Miaotried to flee, but was encircled and hacked topieces.•••

So the Dowager has lost both her brothers, who wielded military power, and the eunuchs, her other arm for effective power, are getting killed. This is not a good day for her

Yuan Shaobade his soldiers scatter and seek out all the families and dependents ofThe Ten, massacring them all regardless of their age. Many beardless men were killed in error in the resultingslaughter.11•••

The dead were said to be more then two thousand. Non-bearded men resorted to exposing themselves to try to avoid being killed.↩

Cao Caoset himself to extinguish the fires in the palace. He then begged Empress Dowager He to undertake the direction of affairs and to send soldiers to pursueZhang Rangand the others and look for the young Emperor.•••

While the Yuan’s go around slaughtering indiscriminately,Cao Caotends to state affairs

***

By thenZhang RangandDuan Guihad hustled awaythe Emperorand thePrinceofChenliu. Bursting through the smoke and fire, they traveled though the night until they reached theBeimang Hills. It was then about the third watch. They heard a great shouting behind them and saw soldiers in pursuit. At their head was anofficerfrom the central division ofHenan commandery,Min Gong, who shouted, “Halt, you traitors!”Zhang Rang, seeing that all was lost, jumped into theYellow Riveranddrowned.12•••

The last words ofZhang Rangand co were “We your servants die, may Your Majesty care for yourself.”↩

Since theEmperorand thePrinceofChenliucould not tell friends from foe amid the fighting, they dared not reveal their location by calling out, but instead lay hidden within the wild grasses along the bank of theYellow River. Soldiers searched for them in every direction, but they could not be found. There they remained until the fourth watch, shivering with cold as dew fell upon them, growing very hungry. There they wept in each other’s arms, silently, lest they be discovered.

Eventually, thePrinceofChenliusaid, “We can’t stay here forever. We’ll have to find another way out.”

Thus the two children knotted their clothes together and managed to climb up the steep bank along the river. Yet they found themselves in a thicket of thorn bushes and, dark as it was, they could not see a path. They began to despair when, all at once, fireflies by the hundreds and thousands sprang up all about them, illuminating the area as they swirled before theEmperor.

“Brother, thus does Heaven aid us!” said thePrince.

Following the fireflies’ light they soon found a road, which they followed. By the fifth watch there their feet ached and they could not continue. Among the hills they spotted a heap of straw, which they crept into and lay down to rest.

On the other side of the heap of straw was a farm house. The farmer inside was just dreaming that a pair of red suns had descended behind his house. He awoke in a start, hurriedly dressed, and went out the door to look around. Out from a heap of straw behind the house he saw a red glow emanating into the heavens. He investigated at once and found the two boys sleeping in the straw.

“To what household do you belong, young gentlemen?” asked he.

TheEmperorcould not muster a reply, but his companion pointed him and said, “He is the reigningEmperor. We fled here to escape violence stirred up by theTen Regular Attendants. I am his younger brother, thePrinceofChenliu.”13•••

The younger prince more assertive and calmer throughout then the elder brother who is supposed to be Emperor.↩

Astonished, the farmer bowed again and again. He said, “I amCui Yi, the younger brother ofHis Late Majesty’sExcellency over the Masses,Cui Lie. Having witnessed howThe Tensold offices and were jealous of worthy people, I’ve been living in seclusion here.”

Cui Yiled theEmperorinto his farm and, kneeling, served his guests with food and drink.

By now,Min Gonghad chased down and capturedDuan Gui. “Where is theSon of Heaven?” he demanded.

“We were separated along the road,”Duan Guisaid. “I don’t know where theEmperorhas gone.”

Min GongkilledDuan Guiand hung the severed head from his horse’s neck. He dispatched his men to search in all directions while he rode off by himself along the road on the same quest. By chance, he stumbled acrossCui Yi’s farm. SeeingDuan Gui’s head dangling from the horse’s neck,Cui YiquestionedMin Gong, who recounted every detail.Cui Yi, satisfied, ledMin Gongto theEmperor. Ruler and minister wept bitterly in thereunion.•••

So after three decades and threes days of slaughter, the eunuchs are destroyed withDuan Guithe last to go. Will the Han be better off for it?

“The state cannot go even one day without its ruler,” saidMin Gong. “I pray,Your Majesty, return to the capital.”

At the farm they had but one sorry nag and this they saddled for theEmperor. Theyoungprincerode together withMin Gongon his own horse. Having left the farm, in less than a mile they fell in with a group of several hundred soldiers under various officials: theExcellency over the Masses,Wang Yun, theGrand Commandant,Yang Biao, theColonel of the Left Army,Chunyu Qiong, theColonel of the Right Army,Zhao Rong, theColonel of the Rear Army,Bao Xin, and theColonel of the Central Army,Yuan Shao. They moved as one to approach theEmperor, with everyone weepingfreely.14•••

Everyone is moved but a poor farm horse, a prince sharing with another is a poor look for a dynasty. Also Palace being on fire and looted by one’s own soldiers.↩

Everyone is moved but a poor farm horse, a prince sharing with another is a poor look for a dynasty. Also Palace being on fire and looted by one’s own soldiers.

A man was sent ahead tothe capitalto issue orders, bearing as a warning the severed head ofDuan Gui.

As swiftly as could be managed, theEmperorand thePrinceofChenliuwere given better steeds. Guarded by the entourage, the youths were escorted back toLuoyang.

Before these events a childrens’ ditty sung inLuoyangforetold:15•••

Children songs and sayings of the public could be seen as omens.↩

Though theEmperordoesn’t rule, though theprinceno office fills,

Yet a brilliant cavalcade comes along fromBeimang Hills.16•••

Beimang Hillswas were the Imperial Family tombs were.↩

The cavalcade had not proceeded far when they saw coming towards them a large body of soldiers with fluttering banners which hid the sun, raising a huge cloud of dust. The officials turned pale and theEmperorwas greatly alarmed.Yuan Shaorode out in advance and demanded who they were.

From under the shade of an embroidered banner rode out an officer who demanded, “Where is theSon of Heaven?”

TheEmperorwas too panic-stricken to respond, but thePrinceofChenliurode to the front and called out, “Who are you?”

“Dong Zhuo, theInspectorofLiang province,” he replied.

“Have you come to protect the chariot, or to steal it?” asked thePrince.

“I have come to protect,” saidDong Zhuo.

“If that is so, theSon of Heavenis here; why do you not dismount?”

Astonished,Dong Zhuohastily dismounted and made obeisance on the left of the road.

Then thePrincespoke graciously to reassureDong Zhuo, and from the first to last of their encounter was never at a loss for what to say.Dong Zhuowas so impressed that he idea of deposingthe Emperorso he may be replaced with thePrinceofChenliuwas already forming in hismind.17•••

The Emperor was nearly of age to rule and his younger brother would require years more of regency. Surely this does not factor into the honesty ofDong Zhuo’s feelings?↩

The Emperor was nearly of age to rule and his younger brother would require years more of regency. Surely this does not factor into the honesty ofDong Zhuo’s feelings?

The cavalcade reached the palace the same day. Therethe Emperorpresented himself to Empress Dowager He, and all wept freely.

However, while the palace was being checked, it was discovered that theHereditary Seal, whose possession confirmed theEmperor’s authority, had gonemissing.•••

This might not be the most positive omen in history

***

Dong Zhuocamped his troops outside the walls ofLuo, and every day he went into the city with an escort of mailed soldiers. They walked up and down the streets, leaving the common people in a state of constant trepidation.Dong Zhuohimself entered and exited buildings in the palace complex as he pleased, showing neither respect or restraint.

Bao Xin,Colonel of the Rear Army, describedDong Zhuo’s behavior toYuan Shaoand warned, “This man harbors some evil design and should be removed atonce.”18•••

Bao Xinwas a cavalry officer underHe Jin, he had gone to raise troops forHe Jinand returned whenHe Jinwas dead↩

“Yet the court has only just been stabilized,” repliedYuan Shao. “Now is not the time for rash decisions.”

Bao Xinthen sawWang Yunand asked what he thought.

“It requires further discussion,” wasWang Yun’s reply.Bao Xin, rebuffed twice, thus took his troops and left for the region aroundMount Tai.•••

Senior ministers at court missing the moment to deal with such a danger, does not reflect well on the court.

Dong Zhuoskillfully induced all the soldiers of the two He brothers, Jin and Miao, to join his command. And privately he said toLi Ru, “I want to remove theEmperorin favor of thePrinceofChenliu. What do youthink?”19•••

Perhaps worth mentioning howDong Zhuowon over the Northern Army. With few troops,Dong Zhuosent some of his men out of the city at night then come in during the morning with banners as if reinforcingDong Zhuoand make his numbers seem larger then they were.↩

“The court is now without a ruler,” explainedLi Ru. “There can be no better time than now to carry out your plan. Delay will spoil everything. Tomorrow, gather the officials in theGarden of Warmth and Brightnessand inform them of your decision. Put all opponents to death, and your prestige is settled.”

Li Ru’s answer delightedDong Zhuo.

The next dayDong Zhuolaid out a grand banquet and invited all the chief ministers. As they were all afraid of him, what could they do but attend?Dong Zhuowaited until all the ministers had arrived, then deliberately, slowly, rode up to the garden last of all and took his place with his sword girded on. Once the wine had gone round several times,Dong Zhuoordered that the service and musiccease.20•••

Wearing a sword in the palace was an executable offense.↩

“I have but one thing to say,” he said harshly. “Listen intently, and do so in silence.”

The assembly awaited his words attentively.

“TheSon of Heaven,” saidDong Zhuo, “is the master of the people. Yet if he lacks dignity and behaves in an unseemly manner; he is not fit to sustain either the altars of the state or the temple of his ancestors. Ourcurrent sovereignis a weakling, inferior to thePrinceofChenliuin intelligence and love of learning. ThePrinceis in every way fitted for the throne. I desire to depose theEmperorand set up thePrincein his place. Ministers, what think you?”

The assembly listened in perfect silence, none daring at first to utter a word ofdissent.•••

This is bold ofDong Zhuo, only at court five minutes and seeking to overthrow the eldest son of the previous Emperor for a much younger candidate. That the senior ministers are intimidated into silence when treason is being proposed is a poor reflection of the court.

Then suddenly one man pushed aside his table and got up, then stood before the feast and shouted, “No, no! Who are you that you dare dictate such bold words? TheSon of HeavenisHis Late Majesty’s son by thelawful consortand has done no wrong. How can you rashly propose that he be replaced? What treason do you seek?”

Dong Zhuosaw that it wasDing Yuan, theInspectorofJing province.2122•••

From poor family with rough manners,Ding Yuanwas a skilled horseman and archer, a valiant fighter who led from the right and would follow orders no matter how difficult.↩

The novel has a loose attitude towards officials meant to be at important provincial posts instead being at capital.↩

Dong Zhuoroared at him, “There is life for those who obey me and there is death for those against me!” he roared.

He drew his sword as though he would beheadDing Yuanat once. But the watchfulLi Ruhad noticed, standing behindDing Yuan, a particularly dangerous-looking henchman of his, who was now handling his halberd threateningly, and whose eyes were ablaze with anger. ThusLi Ruhastily interposed, saying, “Today’s feast is not fit to discuss state affairs. It won’t be too late to go over such public affairs tomorrow.”

The other guests persuadedDing Yuanto mount his horse and leave. Once he had left,Dong Zhuoasked the other ministers, “Isn’t what I said just and reasonable?”

“You have fallen short, Illustrious Sir,” saidLu Zhi. “In ancient times, it was because of the ruler Tai Jia’s lack of wisdom that his chief ministerYi Yinbanished him to the [location I’d="958"]Tong Palace, and in the history of our own Han state, it was because of the more than three thousand wicked acts which thePrince of Changyicommitted in a mere twenty-seven days on the throne that his chief ministerHuo Guanginformed the Ancestral Temple of his misdeeds and stripped him of the imperial title. Ourcurrent sovereignmay be young, but he is intelligent, benevolent and wise, nor has he committed the slightest lapse or fault. Furthermore, you yourself are merely theInspectorof a border province and have never participated in capital affairs, nor do you have the great talents ofYi YinorHuo Guang. How could you press the issue of replacing one sovereign with another? As the sage Mencius said, ‘WithYi Yin’s intention it is permissible, without it isusurpation’.”2324•••

Tai Jia was the fourth King of the Shang dynasty who was deposed by his chief ministerYi Yinand exiled, with Shang ruled via regency, before being allowed to return having changed his cruel ways.↩

Liu He,Prince of Changyi, became Emperor but rather then mourn for his uncle, he enjoyed the good life. This was considered a scandal andHuo Guang, with his grand-daughter as Dowager, deposed the recent Emperor, setting precedent for how a Dowager could depose a young ruler and list of charges to throw at young Emperors one might wish to depose.↩

Infuriated,Dong Zhuodrew his sword to slay the bold speaker, butGentleman ConsultantPeng Boremonstrated.

“Secretariat MinisterLuis held in high regard by all the realm; if you were to kill him, I fear the realm would tremble,” saidPeng Bo.

Dong Zhuo, on this advice, stayed his hand.

Then saidWang Yun, “A great question like the deposition and enthronement of Emperors is not to be decided after a wine party. Let it be put off until another day.”

So the guests dispersed.Dong Zhuostood at the garden gate, gripping the pommel of his sword as he watched them depart.

Standing thus he suddenly noticed a mounted spearman galloping to and fro outside the garden gates. He askedLi Ru, “Who is that?”

“That is the adopted son ofDing Yuan,”Li Rutold him. “He is calledLü Bu, styledFengxian. You must not pursue him, my lord.”Dong Zhuowent inside the gate so that he could not be seen.

However, the following day, someone reported toDong ZhuothatDing Yuanhad led an army outside the city and was challenging him to a battle. Angered,Dong Zhuoled his own troops out withLi Ru. The armies were soon drawn up in proper array to face one another.

Lü Buwas a conspicuous figure among the enemy. His hair was arranged under a handsome headdress of gold and he had donned a beautiful embroidered fighting robe and a pheasant-tail helmet and breast plate, and round his waist was a gleaming jade belt with a lion’s head clasp. Galloping with his halberd at the ready, he accompaniedDing Yuanout to the front of theirlines.•••

Two glimpses at this warrior and now a full, magnificent view, a wonder to behold even before a fight.

Pointing his finger atDong Zhuo,Ding Yuanbegan to revile him.

“It was bad enough for the state when the eunuchs toyed with power and drove the common people to misery. Now you, devoid of the least merit, dare to rashly speak of deposing the rightful Emperor and setting up another. What chaos are you inflicting upon the court?”

BeforeDong Zhuocould reply,Lü Burode straight at him, eager for the fight.Dong Zhuofled in panic, whileDing Yuan’s army swept down upon his own troops.Dong Zhuo’s soldiers were greatly defeated, and the beaten men withdrew more than thirty li and made another camp. HereDong Zhuocalled his officers to a council.

“ThisLü Buis a marvel,” saidDong Zhuo. “If he was only on my side, who else in the realm would be cause for worry?”

At this a man advanced, saying, “Have no fear, my lord! I am a fellow villager ofLü Buand know him well: he is bold but without cunning, and in the pursuit of personal gain he forgets his principles. With my persuasive tongue, I could convince him to willingly defect to your side. How aboutit?”•••

Not the most ringing character endorsement one has ever heard

Delighted,Dong Zhuostudied the speaker and recognized him asLi Su, one of hisGenerals of the Household Rapid Tigers.

“How will you convince him?” askedDong Zhuo.

Li Sureplied, “I’ve heard you have a fine horse called Red Hare, capable of running a thousand li in a single day. I must have this steed, along with gold and pearls, to win his heart. If I then take the opportunity to persuade him,Lü Buwill certainly abandonDing Yuan’s service for yours.”

“What think you?” saidDong ZhuotoLi Ru.

“One cannot begrudge a horse to win an empire,” repliedLi Ru.

Dong Zhuoagreed, and he gave the corrupter of morals what he demanded—a thousand taels of gold, ten strings of lustrous pearls, a jade belt, and the Red Hare—to bring along on his visit to his fellow villager.Li Suaccepted them with due ceremony, then set out forLü Bu’s camp. To the guards patrolling the road he said, “Please tellGeneral Lüat once that a very old friend has come to visit him.”

The guards sent in this report andLü Buordered him to be admitted.

WhenLi Sucame in, he said, “My worthy little brother, have you been well since we last met?”

Lü Bureplied with a salute, “How long it’s been! And where are you now?”

“I’ve been appointed asHousehold General of the Rapid Tigers,”Li Sureplied. “When I learned you were a strong supporter of the throne, I could not contain my joy. I have come now to present to you a really fine horse, a thousand li a day horse, one that crosses rivers and goes up mountains as if walking on a level plain. He is called Red Hare. I present him especially to you, to augment your tiger ferocity.”

Lü Buquickly bade them lead out the horse to see it. The horse was indeed of a uniform color like burning embers; not a single hair of another color. He measured a full zhang from head to tail, and eight chi from hoof to neck. When he neighed the sound reached into the highest heavens and shook the ocean.

A poet of a later age described Red Hare:25•••

Red Hare is thought to be a blood-sweating Dayuan horse with it’s mane and tail seeming fiery red. However the name is thought to be for it’s ability to stop when it spots a hare which was useful for hunting.↩

The steed swift and tireless, spurned by his hoofs the dust rises in clouds;

Now he swims the river, anon climbs the hill, rending the purple mist asunder,

Scornful he breaks the rein, shakes from his head the jeweled bridle,

He is as a fiery dragon descending from the highest heaven.

WhenLü Busaw the horse he was delighted, and expressed his gratitude enthusiastically “Brother, how could I ever repay you for gifting me such a fine steed?”

“What return can I hope for?” askedLi Su. “I came to you in friendship and camaraderie.”

Lü Busent for wine and both drank to their heart’s content.

“Worthy brother, we have seen very little of each other,” saidLi Su, “but your honorable father visits often.”

“Brother, you must be drunk!” explainedLü Bu. “My father has been dead for years. How could you be meeting with him?”

Li Sulaughed and said, “Oh, no, not him. I meant the man of the day,InspectorDing.”

Lü Buflinched. “I am withDing Jianyang, as much is true. But only because I can do no better.”

“Brother, your talent is higher than the heavens, deeper than the seas. Who in all the world does not bow before your name? Fame and riches and honors are yours for the taking. And you say you can do no better than remain this man’s subordinate?”

“If I could only find a worthy master to serve!” saidLü Bu.

Li Sulaughed again and said, “Like they say, ‘the clever bird chooses the branch whereon to perch; the wise servant selects the master to serve’. It’s never too early to look for opportunities, but always too late for regret once they havepassed.”26•••

The wise bird chooses it’s branch, or the wise officer chooses their master, is a common theme.↩

“Well, you are at court. Who do you think is the hero of this age?” askedLü Bu.

Li Sutold him, “I’ve gotten a good look at them all, and none measure up toDong Zhuo. He is one who respects the talented and reveres scholars; he is clear and precise in his rewards and punishments. He’s the one who will accomplish great deeds.”

“I’d follow him if I could,” saidLü Bu. “I only regret that there’s no way to make it happen.”Li Suproduced the pearls and gold along with the jade belt, laying them out before his host.

“What’s this all about?” askedLü Bu.

Li Suordered the attendants away, then explained, “Lord Donghas long respected your valor. He gave me special orders to present these to you. Red Hare was also from him.”

“Yet how am I to repay such affection fromLord Dong?” askedLü Bu.

“If even a stupid fellow like me can rise to becomecommanderof theRapid Tigers, it is impossible to say what honors would await someone like you.”

“If only there was something I could do for him first, some merit to offer as an introduction.”

“On the contrary, there is one service you could perform as easily as turning your hand,” suggestedLi Su, “but I fear you would be unwilling to do it.”

Lü Bupondered long in silence. Then he said, “I might slayDing Yuanand bring his soldiers over toDong Zhuo’s side. What do you think ofthat?”•••

Took quite a few hints fromLi SubutLu Bugot there in the end

“Worthy Brother,” saidLi Su, “if you would do that there could be no greater service! But this matter should not be delayed. You must act quickly.”

Lü BupromisedLi Suthat he would do the deed and return on the morrow.

ThusLi Sutook his leave. That very night, at the second watch,Lü Buentered, sword in hand, into his master’s tent. He foundDing Yuanreading by the light of a solitary candle. Seeing who came in he said, “My son, what is afoot?”

“I am a bold hero,” saidLü Bu, “do you think I am willing to be a son of yours?”

“Why this change of heart,Fengxian?” pleadedDing Yuan.

Lü Bu, already advancing, with a single slice of his blade sentDing Yuan’s head tumbling to the earth. Then he called out to the attendants, saying, “Ding Yuanwas an unjust man and I have slain him. Let those who back me stay; the others maydepart!”•••

While the novel talks of choosing one’s master, killing your adopted father is a big filial no-no. Also not great to kill defender of the Han to help a man seeking to depose the Emperor.

More than half of them scattered. Next day, bearing the head of theDing Yuanas his gift,Lü Buwent to seeLi Su, who led him toDong Zhuo.Dong Zhuoreceived him with a warm welcome and had wine set beforehim.•••

Not a great look forLu Buthat most ofDing Yuan’s men leave him after the murder of their boss.

“General, I welcome you having come to me as the parched grass welcomes dew,” saidDong Zhuoas he made obeisance.

Lü BuhelpedDong Zhuoto seat himself and then made his own obeisance, saying, “If you would have me, sir, pray let me honor you as my adopted father.”

Dong Zhuobestowed upon his newly-won ally golden armor and a brocade battle gown, and they drank heartily together before parting ways.

ThenceDong Zhuo’s power and influence increased even further. He appointed himself as actingGeneral of the Front, and he appointed his younger brotherDong MinasGeneral of the LeftandMarquisHuandLü BuasCavalry Commandant,Household Gentleman, andDistrict Marquisof a capital district. AndLi RuurgedDong Zhuoto swiftly finalize his plans to replacethe Emperorwith thePrinceofChenliu.

Once moreDong Zhuoprepared a feast at the government complex, inviting all the chief ministers. He also badeLü Bupost a company of more than a thousand armored soldiers to stand guard on either side. When the day came, theGrand Tutor,Yuan Wei, and the other ministers arrived. Once the wine had gone round several times,Dong Zhuodrew his sword and declared, “Thecurrent sovereignis weak and irresolute, unfit for the duties of his high place. Thus I plan to imitate the examples ofYi YinandHuo Guangby setting asidethis Emperoras thePrinceofHongnong, and I will place on the throne the presentPrinceofChenliu. Whoever does not go along will be beheaded!”

Fear seized them in its grip and they were silent. ThenYuan Shaodrew himself up and stepped forward to say, “Thecurrent sovereignhas reigned but a short time, and he has shown no lack of virtue. Yet you want to set aside the son of the wife to enthrone the son of the concubine. What else is that but rebellion?”

AndDong Zhuoraged, “I decide the affairs of the realm! If I say it is so, who dares defy it? Doesn’t my sword look sharp to you?”

Yuan Shaoflashed his sword out of its sheath. He said, “Your sword may be sharp, but mine has never been blunt!”

The two men stood face to face amid the feasters.

And so it was:

Ding Yuandied first for the virtuous cause

NowYuan Shaorisks the deadly side

For how the fate ofYuan Shaowill end up, read the next chapter to find out.
